items.title: Training a linear regressor on a small tabular dataset
items.scenario: A team has 10,000 rows and fits ŷ = w·x + b with MSE loss. Each epoch, they run forward and backward on all 10,000 rows to compute ∇L, then apply θ ← θ − η∇L once. Loss decreases smoothly epoch to epoch because the full-batch gradient is exact.
items.explanation: This is textbook batch GD: one gradient over the complete dataset per update. Feasible at 10k rows; impractical at LLM scale without mini-batches.

items.title: Quadratic bowl in one parameter
items.scenario: Loss L(w) = (w − 5)² has minimum at w = 5. Starting at w = 0 with η = 0.1, ∇L = 2(w − 5) = −10, so w ← 0 − 0.1(−10) = 1. Repeating drives w toward 5 with diminishing steps as ∇L → 0.
items.explanation: On a simple convex quadratic, batch GD converges to the unique minimum; step size η determines speed and stability.

items.title: Debugging a model that never improves
items.scenario: An engineer logs loss per epoch during full-batch training. Loss oscillates wildly instead of decreasing. They verify the update uses subtraction (not addition) and try halving η from 1.0 to 0.1.
items.explanation: Oversized η on a steep loss surface causes overshoot; batch GD's exact gradient makes oscillation a pure step-size issue, not mini-batch noise.
